item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As much as I love the transcript, there are a lot of big and obvious holes. Foundation realizes they captured a specific group from a larger society, but they mention nothing about the rest of the society other than, "if uoi find them let us know." There'd be some more discussion or speculation about the society.

Yes, how come they are completely oblivious to their experience during transport? Even if they didn't realize what exactly was happening, they'd notice the bizarre transition.

Finally, unlike many anomalous effects, how these guys hear and speak are thoroughly understood, and a way to appeal with them is obvious. I think absence of communication attempts is glaring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting marine scip but there are a few things preventing me from upvoting. The first is the fact that the Foundation hasn't even attempted to communicate with a fully sentient scip. The second is I feel this article cuts off before we get to the really interesting stuff. I'm all for letting the reader make up their own stories but this one has too little detail to truly fuel my imagination.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the image comes from here with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice. A manta ray democracy, with an interesting religion. Leaves a lot to the imagination, but in a way that does not detract from the article.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was just delightful. Loved the way the rays interacted.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert edits at anytime.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

So, this one kept me stumped for a bit due to just how stand alone it is. While a previous wikiwalk link does bridge SCP-695 to here, a link back doesn't quite work out. This is one of LurkD's Site-45 articles though, so when all else fails, linking to that hub is a non-invasive option that opens readers to the rest of the Site-45 world. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

SCP-1340 is to be held within Containment Aquarium Tank-3 at Research Site-45. Personnel assigned to SCP-1340 are to observe standard aquatic zoological containment protocols. 150kg of food pellets made up of ground krill will be deposited into SCP-1340's containment tank 4 times a week. Voltmeter and galvanometer instrumentation are to be installed within the tank to monitor and decipher fluctuations of electrical activity.

To this:

SCP-1340 is to be held within Containment Aquarium Tank-3 at Research Site-45. Personnel assigned to SCP-1340 are to observe standard aquatic zoological containment protocols. 150kg of food pellets made up of ground krill will be deposited into SCP-1340's containment tank 4 times a week. Voltmeter and galvanometer instrumentation are to be installed within the tank to monitor and decipher fluctuations of electrical activity.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As a Master Mason who was formerly a lodge officer, I found this quite entertaining. The puzzlement of the displaced rays, and their thoughtful discussions, were well depicted.

Still trying to imagine what the initiation ceremony could be like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, this is well written, but it's simply not doing anything for me.

Also, the way the article reads (maybe that's not what was meant to be conveyed), the mantas spontaneously learned to communicate in Spanish just because they were able to receive Spanish radio broadcasts. That's like saying that if you speak English in front of a squirrel long enough, it'll eventually be able to communicate in English. I'd suggest giving at least a quick explanation for the manta's remarkable intelligence and sapience, because that aspect doesn't seem to have been addressed here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't want to like this, I didn't like it at first, I thought I was going to down vote, just too silly, but then I read the log and now i'm an upvote. You win this time, friend.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1340 possess two large electrocyte organs on each side of its head, where current passes from the lower to the upper surface of the body allowing bioelectrogenesis to occur.

This seems like enough for biologists to take note, and if/when they found out about their communication, the Foundation probably intervened.
E: wrong segment

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean, unless you had a skilled cryptologist analyze their electrical signals (and why would anyone but the Foundation do that?) you wouldn't notice anything to distinguish them from basic cave fish, etc. What exactly was wierd enough about these guys to start with that the Foundation got involved?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Explain?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Forgive me if I'm missing something, but why was the Foundation looking into this to begin with?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A manta theocracy. I like it. +1

Also,how did the Foundation relocate them without them noticing? Wouldn't it be easier to just block off their section of the caves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Clever idea, and the log tugs at the heart. I would have +1'd, but honestly, how could the Foundation even have discovered these things? Nobody would even have realized that these were anomalous unless the Foundation did research on their communication in the first place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very well done! Have a +1 and my first comment on this Wiki!

So, I have to agree with others who have asked for expansion on this. Also, an explanation of why all the captured subjects are male would be nice. For example, is their species like octopi where the females die in the process of caretaking their eggs? Or are they simply even more male dominated as a society than we are? Or do their females work as part of other organizations that are among the still at large population?

In any event, I'd be particularly interested to see how they react to female humans in positions of authority.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Mantas clearly do want to get out though. I'm not sure the Foundation would be nice enough to tell them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, poor little… mantas? It's the second time today an scip makes me feel bad about something totally random, well done! Upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe some problem with a swimmer that discovered some imposibly intelligent fish while exploring and underwater cave? There are lots of possible explanations, but all that come to my mind are important enough to be mentioned in the scip, I'll give you that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My only minor quip is that I really cannot imagine how these guys were discovered.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Come on, now, you can't leave me hanging like this! It's a great article, but there's so much more room for development. Why hasn't the Foundation tried to communicate with them? What about the rest of the species - if the Foundation has 118 males, well, there's got to be at least one female somewhere, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to disagree with many of the previous posts saying that this has nothing to distinguish itself. This is a rather original piece, first you have a colony that is aware of its new surroundings but without any idea of why. You have a religious aspect introduced, but balance it with parliamentary procedure. These mantas have some real depth potential, and I would love to see full development. Experimental separation and reintroduction, Spanish AM radio introduced, and the obvious attempt to talk directly to them. This SCP has a significant cost in it's containment, why wouldn't the foundation try to get some work out of them? A full +1 from me, with desire for expansion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad, but it doesn't do enough to distinguish itself from the 80,000 other talking/sapient animals we have in containment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In my experience, Mexican spanish translates very cleanly into English.
I don't know why this is, but is suspect it's due to the extensive cultural cross-polination between Mexico and the USA.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why hasn't the Foundation attempted to communicate with them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: *article

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this a lot for what it is. Sapient, talking animals usually need a lot done to set them apart from the others, and I'm impressed with the fact that you did it with little. You do a great job of fleshing out their collective personalities and society through the interview.

I am going to agree with Soulless however on the clean and verbose translations being a little weird, though. Normally there'd be a few things lost in translation, but that isn't enough to seriously detract from the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the second sentient animal artical I've read so far (the other being 408) and I think this was done with a lot of thought. The fact that it has a society gave enough character for my +1. I'd like to see some tests with these mantas one day perhaps. That would be interesting.

Over all well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: *ahem*…mantas :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Poor stingrays. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I need to read these.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like knowing how SCPs with a unique outlook on the universe react to their containment procedures. +1 for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Me gusta.

Thought this was going to be another "animals what have powers!" thing, but the 35 (now 36) upvotes convinced me to read it out.

Would the foundation consider telling the manta rays what is really going on? I'd think that they'd be nice enough to tell…unless the mantas want to get out one way or another…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've seen this picture on my tumblr a few times, so I was leery of some kind of "nature… but magical/creepy!" content, but this turned out to be very entertaining and interesting. Reminded me a bit of Robert Siegel's whale books, in which humpbacks all speak like Anglo Saxon warrior poets.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This… This is amazing. At first I didn't find it interesting, and then I read the transcript, and then I was laughing, and then they started arguing, and then I was intrigued, and then it reached the end and I was laughing. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback. I will make the appropriate changes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't agree with how the dialogue was translated, since I'm not sure how Spanish translates into English but in what I know of languages they are not usually translated so easily as to leave no discussion on the best word with X connotation for a specific phrase that doesn't exist in English or anything like that. Not even translator's notes? Something like (Translator's Note: "XXXX phrase" spoke in a dialect known only to YYYY area, suggesting possible variation in the source of SCP-XXXX's development of language) or something? IDK, it's a small nitpick honestly and isn't necessary to the article. I just tend to be finicky about such matters and upvoted anyways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To echo Mackenzie, is there any reason why you systematically put commas before names? You do it so consistently that I'm starting to wonder whether there's a logic behind it.

Aside from that, the idea isn't hugely original but it is done very well. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Minor nitpicks:

[…] distinguishable from […]

This should probably be either "distinguishable by" or "distinguished by".

A motion was called by, One Who Feeds Many. Motion was for temporarily […]

You have a lot of places where you have an unnecessary comma before a name. This one in particular could be seamlessly merged with the sentence after it, making it, "A motion (has been/was) called by One Who Feeds Many for temporarily […]".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to no-vote, which is something I very rarely do. I'm going to blame Smapti and his sentient-animal obsession for this.

On the one hand, you've definitely given them a distinct personality in order to counteract the 'talking animals are overdone' problem, but I genuinely can't decide whether or not that personality is enough to make me like it as an article. If was anything other than talking animals with a special, unique cultural personality, I think I'd have upvoted it for the object's shocked and confused response to containment. The mantas themselves don't do much for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for your input.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At first i was all "yawn, another set of sentient animals" but then I was totally charmed by the transcript.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good work improving it from the early draft, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. Has an interesting premise and delivers on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is very good, well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been an admirer of that photo for a long time.
Very funny and sad at the same time
+1
